title: NECO vs WAEC: Which Exam Is Easier and Which Should You Take?
author: Tklesson Team
content:
Every secondary school student in Nigeria eventually asks the same question: is NECO easier than WAEC? The question comes up in classrooms, hostels, and online forums. Some students swear that NECO is simpler and the grading is more generous. Others insist that WAEC is more straightforward. The truth is more nuanced than either camp admits. Both exams test the same syllabus and are accepted by the same universities. But they differ in style, structure, and student experience. Here is an honest comparison to help you decide which exam to prioritize, or whether to take both.

What Makes an Exam Feel Difficult

Before comparing the two exams, it is worth understanding what makes any exam feel difficult. Difficulty is not just about the content. It is about the question style, the grading standards, the time pressure, and the candidate's preparation. An exam that asks straightforward recall questions may feel easier than one that asks application and analysis questions, even if both cover the same content. An exam with stricter grading may feel harder even if the questions are similar. Perceived difficulty is a combination of all these factors, not a single objective measure.

With this in mind, comparing NECO and WAEC requires looking at more than just the syllabus. Both exams are based on the national curriculum. Both test the same subjects with similar topic coverage. The differences lie in how they test, not what they test.

The Case for NECO Being Slightly Less Difficult

Many students report that NECO questions are more direct. A NECO question is more likely to ask you to state, define, or list. A WAEC question is more likely to ask you to explain, discuss, or evaluate. The direct question requires recall. The analytical question requires recall plus application. For a student who has memorized the material but may not fully understand how to apply it, NECO can feel more approachable. The question style rewards factual knowledge, which is easier to acquire through rote study than analytical skill.

NECO is also perceived as more generous in grading. Students who take both exams sometimes report earning a grade or two higher on NECO than on WAEC for the same subject. This perception is difficult to verify because WAEC and NECO do not publish their raw grade boundaries. But the pattern is consistent enough across years and subjects that it likely reflects a real difference in grading standards. NECO appears to set its credit threshold at a slightly lower raw score than WAEC does for many subjects.

There is also a practical consideration. NECO is taken after WAEC, typically in July. A student who has just spent months preparing for WAEC is already well-prepared for NECO. The additional preparation time is minimal because the syllabus is the same. The student essentially takes two shots at the same material. The second shot often goes better, not because the exam is easier, but because the student has had more practice and is less anxious.

The Case for WAEC Being More Straightforward

WAEC has been around longer and its question patterns are more predictable. Past questions going back decades are readily available. Students and teachers know what to expect. The predictability allows for more targeted preparation. A student who has studied ten years of WAEC past questions has a strong sense of what will appear on their exam. NECO, being newer, has a shorter history of past questions. The patterns are less established. The predictability advantage belongs to WAEC.

WAEC is also more widely recognized internationally. For students who plan to study outside Nigeria or West Africa, WAEC is the more familiar credential. Universities in the United Kingdom, the United States, and Canada are more likely to have existing evaluation frameworks for WAEC results. NECO results are also accepted, but they may require additional explanation or verification. The international recognition edge goes to WAEC.

Some students also find WAEC's question style more intellectually satisfying. The emphasis on explanation and analysis rewards deeper understanding. A student who genuinely grasps the material may prefer WAEC because it allows them to demonstrate that understanding. The NECO emphasis on recall can feel limiting to a student who has moved beyond memorization to comprehension.

Which Should You Take?

The ideal strategy for most students is to take both exams. WAEC comes first, typically in May or June. NECO follows in July. The preparation for WAEC prepares you for NECO. Taking both gives you two chances to earn credits in each subject. If you underperform in a subject on WAEC, you can improve on NECO. Universities accept combined results, so a credit in WAEC English and a credit in NECO Mathematics count together toward your five required credits.

If you can only take one, take WAEC. It is the more widely recognized exam, its patterns are more predictable, and it is required by more institutions as a primary credential. But the question of which is easier is less important than the question of which you are better prepared for. A well-prepared student will perform well on either exam. A poorly prepared student will struggle on both. The exam is not the variable. Your preparation is.

The Bottom Line

NECO is often perceived as slightly less difficult than WAEC due to more direct question styles and potentially more generous grading. WAEC benefits from greater predictability and wider international recognition. The best strategy is to take both exams to maximize your chances of earning credits in all required subjects. If you must choose one, choose WAEC. But the most important factor is not which exam you take. It is how well you prepare.
